q-oni-oniannexe4fig46-de851140-c3876778.stem: A sign reads "12 km/h" together with the indication "1000 m". How should it be interpreted?
choices: An obligation not to exceed 12 km/h starting from a distance of 1000 m
A minimum distance of 12 m to be maintained over 1000 km
A depth of 12 m over a channel length of 1000 m
q-oni-oniannexe4fig46-de851140-c3876778.explanation: ONI Annexe 4, fig. 46 — example: obligation not to exceed 12 km/h at 1000 m.

q-oni-oniannexe4fig49-ad8400ee-33ef26ec.stem: How is an isolated obstacle marked?
choices: By a cone point down, painted red or unpainted
By a cone point up, painted green or unpainted
By a cylinder painted red or unpainted
q-oni-oniannexe4fig49-ad8400ee-33ef26ec.explanation: ONI Annexe 4, fig. 49 — isolated obstacles: cone point down painted red or unpainted.

q-oni-oniannexe4fig50-d3e83c17-db7f9c4a.stem: In channel marking, by what marks are its two sides delimited?
choices: Cylinders painted red and cones point up painted green
Cones point down painted red and green cylinders
Yellow floats fitted with radar reflectors
q-oni-oniannexe4fig50-d3e83c17-db7f9c4a.explanation: ONI Annexe 4, fig. 50 — channel: red cylinders and green cones point up.

q-oni-oniannexe4fig52-979bca5a-daf9d185.stem: To mark a shoal near the shore, which marks are used on the open-water side and on the shore side?
choices: Open-water side: cylinders; shore side: cones
Open-water side: cones; shore side: cylinders
Open-water side and shore side: yellow floats
q-oni-oniannexe4fig52-979bca5a-daf9d185.explanation: ONI Annexe 4, fig. 52 — shoal near the shore: open-water side cylinders, shore side cones.

q-oni-oniannexe4fig53-12f3a908-564ca9f5.stem: For a marked channel in a shoal area, how are the right and left sides marked as seen from the open water?
choices: Right side: green cones; left side: red cylinders
Right side: red cylinders; left side: green cones
Both sides: cones point down painted red
q-oni-oniannexe4fig53-12f3a908-564ca9f5.explanation: ONI Annexe 4, fig. 53 — channel in a shoal area: right side (seen from open water) green cones, left side red cylinders.

q-oni-oniannexe4fig54-c3024fd8-7dcd9bb0.stem: How does a cardinal mark indicate the North quadrant of an extended obstacle?
choices: Two cones one above the other, both points up
Two cones one above the other, both points down
Two cones one above the other, the lower cone point down and the upper cone point up
q-oni-oniannexe4fig54-c3024fd8-7dcd9bb0.explanation: ONI Annexe 4, fig. 54 — North quadrant: two cones one above the other, both points up.

q-oni-oniannexe4fig55-ab288aa3-7fd073f9.stem: Cardinal marks indicate an extended shoal by indicating the North and West quadrants. What do they indicate?
choices: That the deep water lies in the North and West quadrants
That the obstacle lies in the North and West quadrants
That passage is prohibited in the North and West quadrants
q-oni-oniannexe4fig55-ab288aa3-7fd073f9.explanation: ONI Annexe 4, fig. 55 — the marks indicate that the deep water lies in the North and West quadrants.

q-rnl-rnlannexeifig10-14b9e7fd-28c54298.stem: At night, which lights must a sailing vessel under motor (sails hoisted or not) carry?
choices: A white masthead light, green and red side lights, and a white stern light
A white light visible from all sides and green and red side lights, without a masthead light
Only an ordinary white light visible from all sides
q-rnl-rnlannexeifig10-14b9e7fd-28c54298.explanation: RNL Annexe I, fig. 10 — white masthead light, green and red side lights, white stern light.

q-rnl-rnlannexeifig11-a62f797a-9ca81c6f.stem: A sailing vessel under motor may, instead of separate side lights and a stern light, combine these lights. How and where is this combined light arranged, in addition to the white masthead light?
choices: A tricolour light combining the side lights and the stern light, placed at the top of the mast or near it
A bicolour light placed in the forward area, with the stern light remaining separate
A white light visible from all sides placed at the stern
q-rnl-rnlannexeifig11-a62f797a-9ca81c6f.explanation: RNL Annexe I, fig. 11 — white masthead light and tricolour light (sides + stern) at the top of the mast or near it.

q-rnl-rnlannexeifig14-3c63d9dd-f83e0153.stem: At night, which lights must a vessel or floating equipment being towed carry?
choices: An ordinary white masthead light and an ordinary white stern light
A white masthead light, green and red side lights, and a white stern light
A single ordinary white light visible from all sides
q-rnl-rnlannexeifig14-3c63d9dd-f83e0153.explanation: RNL Annexe I, fig. 14 — ordinary white masthead light and ordinary white stern light.

q-rnl-rnlannexeifig16-876eb8a5-7c8d85d1.stem: At night, how does a non-motorised vessel navigating alone or in a towed convoy mark itself?
choices: By an ordinary white light visible from all sides
By a white masthead light and green and red side lights
By an ordinary yellow light visible from all sides
q-rnl-rnlannexeifig16-876eb8a5-7c8d85d1.explanation: RNL Annexe I, fig. 16 — ordinary white light visible from all sides.

q-rnl-rnlannexeifig17-98ce7451-f4d4f025.stem: At night, by which light may a vessel under sail (without motor) mark itself in the simplest way?
choices: An ordinary white light visible from all sides
A white masthead light accompanied by green and red side lights
A tricolour light placed at the top of the mast
q-rnl-rnlannexeifig17-98ce7451-f4d4f025.explanation: RNL Annexe I, fig. 17 — ordinary white light visible from all sides.

q-rnl-rnlannexeifig18-71d213c1-d6939ea8.stem: At night, a vessel under sail carrying side lights and a stern light: what are these lights?
choices: Green and red side lights and a white stern light
Green and red side lights, a white stern light and a white masthead light
An ordinary yellow light visible from all sides
q-rnl-rnlannexeifig18-71d213c1-d6939ea8.explanation: RNL Annexe I, fig. 18 — green and red side lights, white stern light (no masthead light).

q-rnl-rnlannexeifig19-5a1f0630-36d63fdc.stem: What does this light signal mean?
choices: Tricolour light placed at the top of the mast or near it
Either a red flag and a white flag
Vessels navigating alone or tugs
q-rnl-rnlannexeifig19-5a1f0630-36d63fdc.explanation: RNL Annexe I – fig. 19 — "Tricolour light placed at the top of the mast or near it".

choices: Additional night marking of priority passenger vessels under way
Pushed convoys
Vessels navigating alone or tugs
q-rnl-rnlannexeifig20-478da848-122cee72.explanation: RNL Annexe I – fig. 20 — "Additional night marking of priority passenger vessels under way".

q-rnl-rnlannexeifig21-b53b2ab6-f516b1c0.stem: At night, how does a vessel that has become unable to manoeuvre signal its situation, in addition to its ordinary lights?
choices: By swinging a red light and/or by sounding the prescribed sound signal
By swinging a white light visible from all sides
By showing a green ball visible from all sides
q-rnl-rnlannexeifig21-b53b2ab6-f516b1c0.explanation: RNL Annexe I, fig. 21 — a red light swung and/or the prescribed sound signal.

q-rnl-rnlannexeifig22-a4ed91e3-c9832330.stem: At night, how may a vessel unable to manoeuvre mark itself by means of fixed lights visible from all sides?
choices: Two red lights one above the other, about 1 m apart, visible from all sides
Two white lights one above the other about 1 m apart
A red light above a white light, both visible from all sides
q-rnl-rnlannexeifig22-a4ed91e3-c9832330.explanation: RNL Annexe I, fig. 22 — two red lights one above the other about 1 m apart, visible from all sides.

q-rnl-rnlannexeifig24-b5accf5f-12f3ae86.stem: At night, how must floating equipment at a standstill be marked?
choices: Lit in such a way that its contours are recognisable
By an ordinary red light above an ordinary white light
By a black ball visible from all sides
q-rnl-rnlannexeifig24-b5accf5f-12f3ae86.explanation: RNL Annexe I, fig. 24 — floating equipment at a standstill lit so that its contours are recognisable.

q-rnl-rnlannexeifig25-3522004d-1396f956.stem: At night, a vessel carrying out works or sounding operations: what does it show on the side where passage is free, and on the side where it is not free?
choices: Free side: ordinary red light above an ordinary white light; non-free side: ordinary red light
Free side: ordinary white light alone; non-free side: ordinary white light above a red light
Free side: green light; non-free side: red light
q-rnl-rnlannexeifig25-3522004d-1396f956.explanation: RNL Annexe I, fig. 25 — free side: ordinary red light and ordinary white light; non-free side: ordinary red light.

q-rnl-rnlannexeifig26-dc7b5242-bedc3637.stem: At night, how are a vessel's anchors marked on the water?
choices: By buoys carrying an ordinary white light
By buoys carrying an ordinary red light
By black balls visible from all sides
q-rnl-rnlannexeifig26-dc7b5242-bedc3637.explanation: RNL Annexe I, fig. 26 — anchors marked by buoys carrying an ordinary white light.

q-rnl-rnlannexeifig27-7ff66719-f5c7379d.stem: By day, by which signal does a priority passenger vessel indicate its priority?
choices: A green ball, visible from all sides
A yellow ball, visible from all sides
A white ball, visible from all sides
q-rnl-rnlannexeifig27-7ff66719-f5c7379d.explanation: RNL Annexe I, fig. 27 — green ball visible from all sides.

q-rnl-rnlannexeifig28-d7ffe00b-222da613.stem: By day, how does a vessel unable to manoeuvre signal its situation by means of a flag?
choices: By swinging a red flag and/or by sounding the prescribed sound signal
By swinging a white flag visible from all sides
By hoisting a green ball visible from all sides
q-rnl-rnlannexeifig28-d7ffe00b-222da613.explanation: RNL Annexe I, fig. 28 — a red flag swung and/or the prescribed sound signal.

q-rnl-rnlannexeifig29-c89b6222-c6604edf.stem: By day, how may a vessel unable to manoeuvre mark itself by means of fixed marks visible from all sides?
choices: Two black balls one above the other, about 1 m apart, visible from all sides
Two green balls one above the other visible from all sides
A white ball visible from all sides
q-rnl-rnlannexeifig29-c89b6222-c6604edf.explanation: RNL Annexe I, fig. 29 — two black balls one above the other about 1 m apart, visible from all sides.

q-rnl-rnlannexeifig30-0b7fee06-d9fb1e5c.stem: By day, a vessel carrying out works: what does it show on the side where passage is free, and on the side where it is not free?
choices: Free side: a red and white flag (or a red flag and a white flag); non-free side: a red flag
Free side: a red flag; non-free side: a red and white flag
Free side: a green ball; non-free side: a black ball
q-rnl-rnlannexeifig30-0b7fee06-d9fb1e5c.explanation: RNL Annexe I, fig. 30 — free side: red and white flag; non-free side: red flag.

q-rnl-rnlannexeifig32-79bb39ae-66f05d59.stem: At night, how does a vessel or floating establishment to be protected against wash mark itself?
choices: By an ordinary red light above an ordinary white light
By an ordinary white light above an ordinary red light
By two red lights one above the other visible from all sides
q-rnl-rnlannexeifig32-79bb39ae-66f05d59.explanation: RNL Annexe I, fig. 32 — at night: ordinary red light above an ordinary white light.

q-rnl-rnlannexeifig35-d034546c-db71770b.stem: What special light do the vessels of the control authorities, fire services and rescue services carry when on a mission?
choices: A scintillating blue light
A bright green light visible from all sides
A scintillating yellow light
q-rnl-rnlannexeifig35-d034546c-db71770b.explanation: RNL Annexe I, fig. 35 — scintillating blue light for vessels on a mission.

q-rnl-rnlannexeifig37-adfe31a5-c4ffb0ae.stem: What flag does a vessel of the control authorities hoist when it wishes to enter into communication with another vessel?
choices: The letter "K" flag
The letter "A" board
A red flag
q-rnl-rnlannexeifig37-adfe31a5-c4ffb0ae.explanation: RNL Annexe I, fig. 37 — letter "K" flag to enter into communication.

q-rnl-rnlannexeifig38-a273c571-a67f81ea.stem: How does a vessel in distress call for help, both at night and by day?
choices: At night: a red light swung in a circle; by day: a red flag or other object swung in a circle
At night: a white light swung; by day: a green ball hoisted
At night: two red lights one above the other; by day: two black balls one above the other
q-rnl-rnlannexeifig38-a273c571-a67f81ea.explanation: RNL Annexe I, fig. 38 — distress: red light (night) or red flag/object (day) swung in a circle.

q-rnl-rnlannexeifig40-0560d461-1141976f.stem: What signal indicates underwater diving carried out from the shore?
choices: A board bearing the letter "A"
A flag bearing the letter "K"
A yellow ball visible from all sides
q-rnl-rnlannexeifig40-0560d461-1141976f.explanation: RNL Annexe I, fig. 40 — letter "A" board for diving from the shore.

q-rnl-rnlannexeifig41-7dd1f930-951aa068.stem: What signal does a vessel used for underwater diving carry when the diving is done from a body of water?
choices: A board bearing the letter "A"
A white ball visible from all sides
A flag bearing the letter "K"
q-rnl-rnlannexeifig41-7dd1f930-951aa068.explanation: RNL Annexe I, fig. 41 — letter "A" board for diving from a body of water.

q-rnl-rnlannexeifig42-12aa6344-8d38ded9.stem: What signal does a vessel trolling and towing one or more floats or spreaders carry?
choices: A white ball, visible from all sides
A yellow ball, visible from all sides
A green ball, visible from all sides
q-rnl-rnlannexeifig42-12aa6344-8d38ded9.explanation: RNL Annexe I, fig. 42 — white ball visible from all sides for trolling.

q-rnl-rnlannexeifig43-9140f412-43e53483.stem: What signal does a professional fishing vessel in operation carry?
choices: A yellow ball, visible from all sides
A white ball, visible from all sides
A green ball, visible from all sides
q-rnl-rnlannexeifig43-9140f412-43e53483.explanation: RNL Annexe I, fig. 43 — yellow ball visible from all sides for professional fishing in operation.

choices: Pushed convoys
Vessels navigating alone or tugs
Professional fishing vessels, motorised pleasure craft
q-rnl-rnlannexeifig7-90f4e02f-ea405173.explanation: RNL Annexe I – fig. 7 — "Professional fishing vessels, motorised pleasure craft".

q-rnl-rnlannexeifig8-928d2012-8bd6725c.stem: At night, which lights may a motorised pleasure craft (or a sailing vessel under motor) carry under this simplified configuration?
choices: A white light visible from all sides and green and red side lights
A white masthead light, green and red side lights and a separate white stern light
An ordinary yellow light visible from all sides
q-rnl-rnlannexeifig8-928d2012-8bd6725c.explanation: RNL Annexe I, fig. 8 — white light visible from all sides and green and red side lights.

choices: Obligation to take the direction indicated by the arrow
Obligation to exercise particular vigilance
Permission to moor
q-rnl-rnlannexeiiifig14-1a7fb6e0-266c607.explanation: RNL Annexe III – fig. 14 — "Obligation to take the direction indicated by the arrow".

choices: Obligation not to exceed the speed indicated (in km/h
Prohibition of all overtaking
Permission to anchor
q-rnl-rnlannexeiiifig15-90a75a42-0dc538c.explanation: RNL Annexe III – fig. 15 — "Obligation not to exceed the speed indicated (in km/h".

choices: Permission for water skiing or the use of similar equipment
Permission to make fast
Permission to anchor
q-rnl-rnlannexeiiifig17-5391f1b7-585160a.explanation: RNL Annexe III – fig. 17 — "Permission for water skiing or the use of similar equipment".

choices: Prohibition of windsurfing
Prohibition of water skiing or the use of similar equipment
Prohibition of parking
q-rnl-rnlannexeiiifig4-4cdd4671-f584c023.explanation: RNL Annexe III – fig. 4 — "Prohibition of water skiing or the use of similar equipment".
